text: The aim of this video is to demonstrate our preferred technique for laparoscopic extended right amicolectomy with the primary vascular approach and radical lymphadenectomy. An eighty-one-year-old lady was investigated for anemia and weight loss, and an adenocarcinoma of the hepatic flexure was diagnosed. Preoperative imaging did not demonstrate evidence of nodal involvement or metastatic disease.

The aim of this video is to demonstrate our preferred technique for laparoscopic extended right amicolectomy with the primary vascular approach and radical lymphadenectomy. An eighty-one-year-old lady was investigated for anemia and weight loss, and an adenocarcinoma of the hepatic flexure was diagnosed. Preoperative imaging did not demonstrate evidence of nodal involvement or metastatic disease.

Following the establishment of pneumoperitoneum using an open technique, the surgeon inserts two additional 5mm ports under visual guidance in the suprapubic and epigastric regions. A fourth 12mm operating trocar is placed in the left iliac fossa.

Following the establishment of pneumoperitoneum using an open technique, the surgeon inserts two 5mm ports under direct vision in the suprapubic and epigastric regions, along with a 12mm operating trocar in the left iliac fossa.
The surgeon employs a medial to lateral no-touch technique, retracting the ileocecal junction laterally to expose the ileocolic vessels for dissection.

The surgeon employs a medial to lateral no-touch technique, retracting the ileocecal junction laterally to expose the ileocolic vessels. A mesenteric window is opened below these vessels, revealing the duodenum and developing the right retrocolic space along the avascular plane of Tholt. The retrocecal appendix is visible. The surgeon approaches the ileocolic pedicle, using a Thunderbit vessel sealing device to dissect and ligate the ileocolic vein and artery separately at the origin of the exposed superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon opens the mesenteric window below the ileocolic vessels, identifying the duodenum and developing the right retrocolic space cranially and laterally along the avascular plane of Toldt. A retrocecal appendix is visible. The surgeon approaches the ileocolic pedicle, using the Thunderbeat vessel sealing device to dissect and separately ligate the ileocolic vein and artery at their origin near the exposed superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to progressively separate the duodenum from the transverse colon, ensuring the duodenum remains visible in the operative field to prevent thermal injury.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to progressively separate the duodenum from the transverse colon, ensuring the duodenum remains visible in the operative field to avoid thermal injury.
The surgical field shows the absence of the right colic artery during the dissection process.
The surgical field shows the absence of the right colic artery in the patient, indicating anatomical variation during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the right branches of the middle colic vessels and ligates them separately at their origin, located on the superior surface of the pancreas.
The surgeon dissects the right branches of the middle colic vessels and ligates them separately at their origin, located on the superior surface of the pancreas.

The surgeon completes the procedure by fully mobilizing the hepatic flexure from below, adhering to the no-touch technique principles. The right colon is mobilized laterally after the vascular supply has been interrupted.

The surgeon completes the procedure by fully mobilizing the hepatic flexure from below. Following the no-touch technique, the right colon is mobilized laterally only after the vascular supply has been interrupted.
The surgical specimen is extracted through a five-centimeter right-sided transverse incision, with wound protection in place. An unsewn end-to-side aliotransverse anastomosis is then constructed.
The surgical specimen is delivered through a five-centimeter right-sided transverse incision. Wound protection is applied, and an unsewn end-to-side aliotransverse anastomosis is fashioned.

The post-operative recovery was uneventful and the patient was discharged home on post-operative day four. Histopathology demonstrated a T-free cancer with none out of twenty-two lymph nodes involved.
